item.title: New Co-Op Witcher Game Details Leaked
item.description:
A new leak is sharing more details about an in-development co-op game set in the universe of The Witcher , taking place before the main story of the franchise. The Witcher spin-off will feature full character customization and skill-based progression.

The fantasy adventure series from CD Projekt Red has cemented itself as one of the pinnacles of the RPG genre throughout its storied history. Based on the novels of the same name, The Witcher has received three main-series games since the release of The Witcher in 2007, alongside a multitude of expansions and spin-offs. CD Projekt Red has even teamed up with board game company Go On Board to develop multiple board games based on The Witcher , most recently crowdfunding more than $14 million for The Witcher: Legacy . Now, new leaks are hinting at a co-op spin-off of the beloved franchise.

The Witcher Co-Op Spin-Off Leaked

  • Co-op multiplayer
  • Character customization
  • Skill system for combat

A recent unsourced leak reported by MP1st provided more details about an upcoming co-op spin-off of The Witcher . The game is said to be focused on mobile platforms and PCs, with the co-op Witcher adventure reportedly not receiving a console release. The spin-off, set in the year 1230, will put players in control of their own customizable Witcher and offers extensive character creation options. The Witcher spin-off will feature a primarily skill-based progression system, allowing players to employ abilities from different Witcher schools and customize their builds to their liking. No official release window was confirmed for the Witcher spin-off within the leak.

Rearrange the covers into the correct US release order.

Your Order

Results

—

—

—

More Games
Rearrange the covers into the correct US release order.
Easy (5)Medium (7)Hard (10)

Your Order

Results

—
— —
More Games

The potential co-op Witcher game has already seen plenty of rumors in recent years. CD Projekt Red has previously confirmed that the studio is working on a spin-off of the series, given the working title "Project Sirius," said to be focused on multiplayer gameplay. The leak did not confirm whether the new game is a reboot of Project Sirius, though the two do share several similarities. The Witcher studio also revealed in early 2025 that it would be partnering with Monopoly GO and Pokemon GO publisher Scopely to create a new game for one of its IPs.

A Witcher spin-off would have plenty of room to differentiate itself from the main-series games within its world. The Witcher games include plenty of iconic locations that could be re-visited in the spin-off, including landmarks around White Orchard, Velen, and Novigrad. The game could even expand beyond places featured within the main-series games, allowing fans to explore other parts of the Witcher universe. The co-op oriented gameplay will likely make the game's combat significantly different from previous games, encouraging teamwork between players.

Who’s That Character?

Identify the silhouettes before time runs out.

5.0 s
0 / 10

Results

0

High Score: 0

—

More Games
Who’s That Character? Identify the silhouettes before time runs out.
Easy (7.5s)Medium (5.0s)Hard (2.5s)Permadeath (2.5s)
5.0 s
0 / 10
Results
0
High Score: 0 —
More Games

The Witcher fans should have lots to look forward to in the near future, with the next main game in the works. CD Projekt Red shared its first official reveal trailer for The Witcher 4 at the 2024 Game Awards, revealing that Ciri will be the protagonist in the next game. The studio also confirmed that the next Witcher trilogy will likely not receive DLC expansions like The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt , with the games planned to release over a six-year time period. The Witcher franchise is gearing up to enter a new era, with lots of big changes on the way for the beloved series.

Subscribe to the newsletter for Witcher co-op leak coverage

Want deeper coverage? Subscribe to the newsletter for focused reporting on the Witcher co-op spin-off and related development leaks, ideal for readers tracking this project and other game industry news.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Source: MP1st

item.title: Dead by Daylight: Best Builds for The Ghost Face
item.description:
Updated on June 14, 2026, by Mark Sammut: Behaviour Interactive shook up the DBD meta with the 9.6.0 update, impacting Ghost Face builds in the process. While the original recommendations are still largely valid, we swapped out a few perks and add-ons to optimize them further.

Dead by Daylight has welcomed dozens of icons from horror history, covering movies, TV shows, and games. From Stranger Things ' The Demogorgon to Resident Evil 's Nemesis and The Ring 's Sadako, a few of the all-time greatest and most powerful villains are currently available as part of the game, and most of them are pretty incredible (and somewhat difficult to master).

Then, there is The Ghost Face , aka Danny Johnson. Hailing from Scream , the killer is an ordinary human in the movie, albeit one who has a twisted sense of humor and a taste for blood. In theory, the DBD version should not be all that amazing, but this Killer certainly has his fans and is quite popular. Admittedly, you will need to spend some time with The Ghost Face to grow accustomed to his strengths and weaknesses, but he is worth the investment.

If this is your first time playing as Danny, you will need to prepare for his non-existent anti-loop game, and the best way to do so is to pick complementary perks that help cover up his blind spots. This article goes through a few of the best Ghost Face builds , hopefully to set you on the right path. We have a beginner build guide, along with one for more veteran players with higher prestige levels.

Has The Ghost Face Undergone Any Changes In 2026?

Dead by Daylight underwent major changes in 2026, with Behaviour Interactive introducing/altering two mechanisms in a way that greatly impacts most Killers. Firstly, DBD 's 9.6.0 update altered modifier stacking, introducing a diminishing returns system. If a build revolves around Powers, Items, Perks, and Offerings that give the same benefits, they will no longer stack completely, with each new one having less of an impact. The 2nd modifier is only 50% effective, the third is only 25%, the fourth is only 12.5%, etc.

The second big change is that a Killer's identity remains hidden until they are spotted/revealed for the first time. This one arguably impacts Ghost Face more than the stacking switch due to being a stealth Killer who benefits greatly from catching Survivors off-guard.

Beyond these widespread changes, DBD also changed Danny in two ways, improving his overall functionality.

  • Ghost Face's Night Shroud now has a cooldown period of 15 seconds, rather than the previous 17 seconds.
  • The Walleye's Matchbook add-on was nerfed slightly, as it now decreases Night Shroud's cooldown by just 2 seconds rather than 3.
  • Ultimately, Ghost Face came out on top.

The Ghost Face: Best Beginner Build

Getting Into The Chase

Without a great range of powerful base perks, The Ghost Face can be a little intimidating for newer players. Luckily, The Ghost Face comes with useful information perks like Thrilling Tremors, which allows him to see progressed generators from afar, and I'm All Ears, which is an excellent chase perk. This build concentrates on pressuring these generators and chasing Survivors from them while not letting them recover.

  • Thrilling Tremors (The Ghost Face) – After picking up a Survivor, all generators not being repaired by Survivors are blocked by the Entity for a certain period. This effect has a cooldown.
  • I’m All Ears (The Ghost Face) – Every 40 seconds, when a Survivor fast vaults within the killer's vicinity, you see their aura for a brief period.
  • Hex: No One Escapes Death (General Perk) – After the final generator is completed, all Survivors suffer from the Exposed status ailment, and the killer gains the Haste effect until the Hex Totem is cleansed.
  • Fearmonger / Mindbreaker (General Perk) – Prevents Survivors from recovering from Exhaustion while repairing generators. (Used to be known as Mindbreaker, nowadays goes by Fearmonger)

Sloppy Butcher also works as a general perk, but it isn't as effective as it used to be since abilities no longer stack efficiently. It causes basic attacks to cause the Mangled and Hemorrhage status ailments, slowing down healing by 25% and regressing healing progress when left unfinished.

The Ghost Face: Best Build

Making Up For Danny's Weaknesses

The Ghost Face doesn't have the best mobility to close the distance on Survivors quickly. He also lacks any innate slowdown in his kit, which makes aura-reading and generator regression perks a must in his best builds.

Opting for Lethal Pursuer to quickly find a few Survivors that can be marked to 99% is ideal. While Nowhere To Hide and Pop Goes The Weasel synergize better with high mobility killers, the Ghost Face greatly benefits from the aura-reading the former provides, which will be further buffed by Lethal Pursuer. Scourge Hook: Pain Resonance is a safe and reliable option for the final perk slot to have consistent pressure on the map.

  • Lethal Pursuer (The Nemesis) – At the start of the trial, the 3 generators furthest from the killer's starting location are blocked for an extended duration or until the first survivor is downed.
  • Surge (The Demogorgon) – When your Basic Attacks put a Survivor in a dying state, generators within 32 meters begin to automatically regress.
  • Friends 'Til the End (The Good Guy) – Hook any Survivor to expose the obsession and reveal their aura. If you hook the obsession, a random survivor will reveal the location and become the new obsession.
  • Scourge Hook: Pain Resonance (The Artist) – The Killer starts with four tokens. For each different Survivor hooked on a white Scourge Hook, lose one token and explode the generator with the most progress, causing it to lose massive progress.

For a more general Killer build that works really well for M1 runs, swap Surge and Friends 'Til the End with Nowhere to Hide and Pop Goes the Weasel. The former activates by kicking a generator, revealing the auras of nearby survivors. The latter is fantastic for regressing a generator's progress, although you first need to hook a survivor and then kick the generator.

The Ghost Face: Best Add-Ons

For players looking to strengthen The Ghost Face’s Night Shroud power, various add-ons can help. Enhancing different aspects of his abilities, this Best Add-Ons section details the strongest Add-Ons to use on The Ghost Face — in order of rarity on the Bloodweb.

“Philly”

Despite being a Common Add-On, “Philly” is among The Ghost Face’s most consistently impactful. This Night Shroud Add-On reduces the time it takes to Mark a Survivor by 10% , meaning you can more quickly Expose Survivors as you stalk them, wasting less time and making you less likely to be Revealed in the act.

Walleye’s Matchbook

An uncomplicated Ghost Face Add-On, the Walleye’s Matchbook, reduces the recovery cooldown on Night Shroud by 2 seconds , allowing you to reenter your power a little sooner after it ends. The default Night Shroud cooldown lasts 15 seconds.

Olsen's Address Book

Olsen's Address Book is a pretty decent Uncommon rarity add-on considering the effect it provides. A full five seconds of wall hacks when chasing a Marked Survivor can be a game-changer in getting downs and maintaining the tempo of the game. It also synergizes very well with Lethal Pursuer, which is part of the Ghost Face's best build.

Olsen’s Wallet

To get back into Night Shroud faster, Olsen’s Wallet may be more appealing than Walleye’s Matchbook. This Rare Add-On immediately recharges The Ghost Face’s power upon breaking a pallet or wall, giving you more opportunities to Mark Survivors mid-chase.

Drop-Leg Knife Sheath

Another one of The Ghost Face’s most powerful Add-Ons, the Drop-Leg Knife Sheath gives the Killer a 10% Haste effect for 5 seconds after Marking a Survivor. Complimenting perks like The Shape’s Play with Your Food, this add-on gives Survivors less time to react before you can land a hit.

Driver’s License

When generators are getting finished too fast, The Ghost Face’s Driver’s License explodes generators being worked on by Survivors who are being marked and blocks them for 15 seconds . This tremendous buff forcefully slows down the game, and it works well with other regression perks like Scourge Hook: Pain Resonance and Pop Goes The Weasel.

Outdoor Security Camera

If finding Survivors isn’t your specialty, the Outdoor Security Camera can help supplement these skills by revealing all Survivors’ auras for 7 seconds after you down a Marked Survivor. This ability allows you to better assess what generators are being worked on and where you need to apply pressure. It also helps avoid flashlights and pallet saves from nearby Survivors, and it can assist in snowballing the game in your favor.

Night Vision Monocular

In 2026, Dead by Daylight increased the importance of a Killer remaining hidden in the early game, as Survivors will not know their identity until they expose them for the first time. This very rare add-on ensures that Ghost Face's reveal comes with consequences for the Survivor, as they will be Exhausted for 10 seconds. Consequently, they won't be able to use distance-closing perks like Lithe or Sprint Burst.

Victim's Detailed Routine is also an option, as it causes 5 seconds of Exhaustion when you mark a Survivor. Don't go for both of these add-ons, as they become redundant.

How To Unlock Daniel Johnson/The Ghost Face In Dead By Daylight

As is the case with all Dead by Daylight 's licensed characters, Ghost Face is not readily available as one of the free characters in any version of the game. Therefore, if you want to play as Danny, you will need to spend extra money to unlock them. Now, compared to other DLC packages, Chapter 12: Ghost Face is a bit different, as the package does not include a Survivor. To date, only this chapter and Chapter 30: Chucky have launched with just a Killer, and the price reflects that.

So, how do you unlock Ghost Face? Well, you just have to purchase Chapter 12, which costs 500 Auric Cells (or $4.99) on both PC and consoles. As a licensed character, he cannot be unlocked using Iridescent Shards, nor is he part of any bundle. Ghost Face is also not included in Dead by Daylight: Gold Edition ​​​​​.

Is The Ghost Face A Good Dead By Daylight Killer For Beginners?

Dead by Daylight has so many Killers at this point that any (relative) newcomers looking to pick their first will be frankly spoiled for choice. On paper, too many options should be a great thing, as it ensures there will be somebody to suit everyone's fancy. However, unless you are just looking to purchase the majority of the roster, most beginners will likely either stick to free characters or settle for a handful of DLC Killers. Should Danny be one of your first purchases if you are trying to get into Dead by Daylight ?

Honestly, every Killer is technically viable for newcomers. Sure, you will need to spend way more time mastering complex Killers like The Nurse or The Hillbilly than beginner-friendly characters like The Wraith or Albert Wesker, but the journey can be a lot more fun if you jump headfirst into the former. The pay-off is also great in both of those cases, particularly The Nurse.

The Ghost Face is a bit of a strange case, as he is a relatively straightforward Killer who relies on stealth and uses his standard melee strike to deal damage. His fundamentals and builds do not sound all that complex in theory, but he is surprisingly difficult to use effectively. He is not a great chaser, as his core method revolves around stalking Survivors. This type of gameplay generally requires players to be fairly familiar with not only a map's layout, but also traditional Survivor builds so that they can accurately predict their actions. Consequently, Danny is not great for beginners, even though he is a relatively simple Killer.

Is The Ghost Face A Good Killer To Main In 2026?

Now that we have discussed whether Ghost Face is accessible for beginners, we have to answer the most important question: Should anybody bother using him?

Before proceeding any further, I wish to just say that if you want to play as Ghost Face because you love Scream , go ahead and do so. It doesn't matter whether he is the best or worst Killer in Dead by Daylight .

On February 27, 2026, Scream 7 hit theaters, which sparked a rise in popularity for Danny in Dead by Daylight . The game didn't really take advantage of the film's release by updating the character in any way, so Ghost Face steadily returned to his average. Currently, he is just out of the top 10 most used Killers, which is by no means a bad spot to be in. In fact, this ranking reflects Ghost Face's 2026 status: He is viable, but he is not the meta Killer .

In low-level matches where the goal is just to unleash a few jumpscaes, Ghost Face works well, as he punishes Survivors with poor situational awareness or teamwork. In competitive, high-level games, Danny can hit the mark, as long as he is in the hands of a skillful player who knows exactly what to do. Even then, there are better options.

Ghost Face Is An M1 Killer

M1 Killers are characters who rely on their basic attack to take out Survivors, with their special ability being secondary. While these Killers can be used effectively in the right hands, they simply have a more limited toolset at their disposal, making them far easier to counter or avoid. Ghost Face works well enough on small indoor maps like "The Game," but he is largely out of his depth in larger ones like Mother's Dwelling.

Ghost Face's Stealth Mode and Stalk Meter

Ghost Face's special ability, Night Shroud, triggers Stealth Mode, making him undetectable as long as he doesn't perform an attack or is spotted by Survivors. Once within 40 meters of a target, players can start to press the Power button to fill Ghost Face's Stalk Meter at 20 pts per second when standing or moving or 40 pts per second when leaning out of cover. The latter is the one you will want to use and master. Once the meter reaches 100 pts, the Survivor will be marked and Exposed, allowing Ghost Face to kill them in one hit for a minute. Marked Survivors also cannot reveal the Killer.

A common strategy is to fill the meter to 99% and then stop. After that, sneak behind the Survivor, tap the Stalk Meter, and then instantly kill them.

Alternative Killers That Are Better Than Ghost Face (In 2026)

Hopefully, Ghost Face will always be, at least, decent enough to be usable on a couple of maps; however, we cannot pretend that he is an A-tier Killer. Simply put, there are too many better options in 2026, ones that offer stealth, speed, and versatility.

  • The Shape – After a revamp toward the end of 2025, Mike Myers went from a joke to one of the better M1 Killers, if not downright the best. At Tier 3, The Shape puts pressure on all Survivors, while Ghost Face can only target one at a time. That said, Danny is a better stealth option.
  • The Wraith – On large maps that expose Ghost Face's slow movement, The Wraith is a more viable candidate. He moves extremely quickly and remains Undetectable, allowing him to patrol generators effectively. However, he is not a one-hit kill option like Ghost Face can be in expert hands.
  • The Ghoul – Arguably, this Killer has become Ghost Face's replacement, offering a playstyle that is a more aggressive take on stealth. Kagune Leap provides great mobility and enables Ken to close distances quickly.

Dead by Daylight

Follow
Followed
Horror
Survival
Systems
OpenCritic Reviews
Top Critic Avg: 70 /100 Critics Rec: 48%
Released
June 14, 2016
ESRB
M for Mature: Blood and Gore, Intense Violence, Strong Language
Developer(s)
Behaviour Interactive

WHERE TO PLAY

PHYSICAL

Driver’s License

When generators are getting finished too fast, The Ghost Face’s Driver’s License explodes generators being worked on by Survivors who are being marked and blocks them for 15 seconds . This tremendous buff forcefully slows down the game, and it works well with other regression perks like Scourge Hook: Pain Resonance and Pop Goes The Weasel.

Outdoor Security Camera

If finding Survivors isn’t your specialty, the Outdoor Security Camera can help supplement these skills by revealing all Survivors’ auras for 7 seconds after you down a Marked Survivor. This ability allows you to better assess what generators are being worked on and where you need to apply pressure. It also helps avoid flashlights and pallet saves from nearby Survivors, and it can assist in snowballing the game in your favor.

Night Vision Monocular

In 2026, Dead by Daylight increased the importance of a Killer remaining hidden in the early game, as Survivors will not know their identity until they expose them for the first time. This very rare add-on ensures that Ghost Face's reveal comes with consequences for the Survivor, as they will be Exhausted for 10 seconds. Consequently, they won't be able to use distance-closing perks like Lithe or Sprint Burst.

Victim's Detailed Routine is also an option, as it causes 5 seconds of Exhaustion when you mark a Survivor. Don't go for both of these add-ons, as they become redundant.

How To Unlock Daniel Johnson/The Ghost Face In Dead By Daylight

As is the case with all Dead by Daylight 's licensed characters, Ghost Face is not readily available as one of the free characters in any version of the game. Therefore, if you want to play as Danny, you will need to spend extra money to unlock them. Now, compared to other DLC packages, Chapter 12: Ghost Face is a bit different, as the package does not include a Survivor. To date, only this chapter and Chapter 30: Chucky have launched with just a Killer, and the price reflects that.

So, how do you unlock Ghost Face? Well, you just have to purchase Chapter 12, which costs 500 Auric Cells (or $4.99) on both PC and consoles. As a licensed character, he cannot be unlocked using Iridescent Shards, nor is he part of any bundle. Ghost Face is also not included in Dead by Daylight: Gold Edition ​​​​​.

Is The Ghost Face A Good Dead By Daylight Killer For Beginners?

Dead by Daylight has so many Killers at this point that any (relative) newcomers looking to pick their first will be frankly spoiled for choice. On paper, too many options should be a great thing, as it ensures there will be somebody to suit everyone's fancy. However, unless you are just looking to purchase the majority of the roster, most beginners will likely either stick to free characters or settle for a handful of DLC Killers. Should Danny be one of your first purchases if you are trying to get into Dead by Daylight ?

Honestly, every Killer is technically viable for newcomers. Sure, you will need to spend way more time mastering complex Killers like The Nurse or The Hillbilly than beginner-friendly characters like The Wraith or Albert Wesker, but the journey can be a lot more fun if you jump headfirst into the former. The pay-off is also great in both of those cases, particularly The Nurse.

The Ghost Face is a bit of a strange case, as he is a relatively straightforward Killer who relies on stealth and uses his standard melee strike to deal damage. His fundamentals and builds do not sound all that complex in theory, but he is surprisingly difficult to use effectively. He is not a great chaser, as his core method revolves around stalking Survivors. This type of gameplay generally requires players to be fairly familiar with not only a map's layout, but also traditional Survivor builds so that they can accurately predict their actions. Consequently, Danny is not great for beginners, even though he is a relatively simple Killer.

Is The Ghost Face A Good Killer To Main In 2026?

Now that we have discussed whether Ghost Face is accessible for beginners, we have to answer the most important question: Should anybody bother using him?

Before proceeding any further, I wish to just say that if you want to play as Ghost Face because you love Scream , go ahead and do so. It doesn't matter whether he is the best or worst Killer in Dead by Daylight .

On February 27, 2026, Scream 7 hit theaters, which sparked a rise in popularity for Danny in Dead by Daylight . The game didn't really take advantage of the film's release by updating the character in any way, so Ghost Face steadily returned to his average. Currently, he is just out of the top 10 most used Killers, which is by no means a bad spot to be in. In fact, this ranking reflects Ghost Face's 2026 status: He is viable, but he is not the meta Killer .

In low-level matches where the goal is just to unleash a few jumpscaes, Ghost Face works well, as he punishes Survivors with poor situational awareness or teamwork. In competitive, high-level games, Danny can hit the mark, as long as he is in the hands of a skillful player who knows exactly what to do. Even then, there are better options.

Ghost Face Is An M1 Killer

M1 Killers are characters who rely on their basic attack to take out Survivors, with their special ability being secondary. While these Killers can be used effectively in the right hands, they simply have a more limited toolset at their disposal, making them far easier to counter or avoid. Ghost Face works well enough on small indoor maps like "The Game," but he is largely out of his depth in larger ones like Mother's Dwelling.

Ghost Face's Stealth Mode and Stalk Meter

Ghost Face's special ability, Night Shroud, triggers Stealth Mode, making him undetectable as long as he doesn't perform an attack or is spotted by Survivors. Once within 40 meters of a target, players can start to press the Power button to fill Ghost Face's Stalk Meter at 20 pts per second when standing or moving or 40 pts per second when leaning out of cover. The latter is the one you will want to use and master. Once the meter reaches 100 pts, the Survivor will be marked and Exposed, allowing Ghost Face to kill them in one hit for a minute. Marked Survivors also cannot reveal the Killer.

A common strategy is to fill the meter to 99% and then stop. After that, sneak behind the Survivor, tap the Stalk Meter, and then instantly kill them.

Alternative Killers That Are Better Than Ghost Face (In 2026)

Hopefully, Ghost Face will always be, at least, decent enough to be usable on a couple of maps; however, we cannot pretend that he is an A-tier Killer. Simply put, there are too many better options in 2026, ones that offer stealth, speed, and versatility.

  • The Shape – After a revamp toward the end of 2025, Mike Myers went from a joke to one of the better M1 Killers, if not downright the best. At Tier 3, The Shape puts pressure on all Survivors, while Ghost Face can only target one at a time. That said, Danny is a better stealth option.
  • The Wraith – On large maps that expose Ghost Face's slow movement, The Wraith is a more viable candidate. He moves extremely quickly and remains Undetectable, allowing him to patrol generators effectively. However, he is not a one-hit kill option like Ghost Face can be in expert hands.
  • The Ghoul – Arguably, this Killer has become Ghost Face's replacement, offering a playstyle that is a more aggressive take on stealth. Kagune Leap provides great mobility and enables Ken to close distances quickly.

Dead by Daylight

item.title: Interview: Pokemon Company Esports Director Talks NAIC's Future, Chicago Move, and Competitive Growth
item.description:
The Pokemon North America International Championships have become much more than a weekend for the best players in the world to chase a spot at Worlds. The 2026 NAIC took place in New Orleans from June 12–14, bringing Pokemon competitors and fans together near the end of the Championship Series season, but the event is already looking toward what comes next as The Pokemon Company prepares to move NAIC to Chicago.

GameRant participated in a group interview at this year's Pokemon NAIC with Chris Brown, Director of Global Esports and Events Producer for The Pokemon Company International. During the conversation, Brown discussed why Chicago gives NAIC room to grow, how Pokemon is balancing serious competition with family-friendly fan experiences, and why accessibility remains such a major part of the company's approach to competitive play.

The following transcript has been edited for brevity and clarity.

Why Pokemon NAIC Is Moving to Chicago

Q: With Pokemon 's footprint already big, with Gofest and the Fossil Museum, is the move to Chicago mainly floor space and capacity, or is there something about Chicago specifically that makes it really the place to be?

A: When we look at events, there are a lot of factors, and we look at airline access to that. New Orleans, by the way, has been fantastic for that. Chicago, just as good, maybe even better. Chicago is super accessible. Most places, major hubs around the world travel direct into Chicago. So that's a big part of this event. We have 48 countries and regions represented this weekend, for example, so having that accessibility is vital now for a show of this scale.

We look for things to do. Our fans, they come in for a competition, but what else is there for them to do? New Orleans, again, checks a ton of boxes. Chicago's very much the same, it's a world-class city. It's going to give our attendees so much to do beyond Pokemon , and we hope they come for the event and enjoy the show, compete and do their best, but at the end of the day, you need to get dinner somewhere. Chicago is a fantastic location that we think you could extend a vacation on either end of our event on.

We are also looking to grow. This venue, we had a 3-year deal, and I think I shared some of the growth metrics. We've been able to grow from when we moved to this venue for the first time 3 years ago to where we are now, we're up 150+%, in terms of what our attendance is. The shocking thing is we actually kind of reached our 3-year goal in 2 years. And so this year everything's sold out. We weren't really able to grow that event. It's going to be pretty flat year over year, not because the demand's not there. The demand is there.

We sold out a lot of our competitions in seconds, but we don't have the space. And it's not because this convention center doesn't have space. It's one of the largest convention centers in the world, really, especially for continuous space. But you have to get far enough out, book the halls, and the halls adjacent to us are booked by other clients. So, Chicago, for us, lets us grow the space, which, for us, we want to grow the competition. We also want to grow our fan programming.

So, everything we're doing that isn't for people that are competing, we want to continue to grow that. That's really where we've been limited for the last couple of years for this show. We've not been able to grow that fan programming because we've prioritized the competitive side and keeping that where it's at. Chicago checks all the boxes. Gives us growth for competitors, gives us growth for fans. It's a great vacation spot for families to attend, and it's extremely accessible for international guests.

Q: NAIC, next year, will come off of Worlds and XP. So, I'm curious if any of the sort of ideas that are going to be in play at Worlds and XP are going to be moved forward. Are there things that you're trying with Worlds and XP that will then become part of the IC identity?

A: We're always experimenting with events. We kind of go through that build, measure, learn loop, and, fortunately, we're able to test things at the ICs and bring them to Worlds and test things at Worlds and bring them to the ICs. I think the component that we've started testing at the international level that I think PXP is really going to break the most ground for is the exhibitor booth and the partner booth space.

If you were at EUIC, you may have seen for the first time we had this great Pokemon TCG Pocket booth. I'm not making any promises to what's at XP or anything, I'm just alluding to this concept of, these shows are so large now, this idea that there's booth space that our partners who are close to the brand can come in and activate and really meet the fan 1 to 1, it's a great experience for our partners. It's a great experience for our fans. So, I'm hoping that that's the next evolution of what we're able to offer at the IC level that isn't currently at this show.

Q: How do you balance creating a high-level competitive environment while still making the event welcoming for casual fans and families?

A: I can't ignore the history. For us, very fortunately, we've been running Pokemon for... we're a generational brand now. And that includes the World Championships, which TPCI first operated in 2004. So, the backbone of that event infrastructure starts from the competitive core, so we're fortunate in that we're grounded there, and then it really becomes a question of how we expand for the whole brand, for all fans, while staying true to what is important for our competitors.

In terms of balancing it, there's not a great answer, so much as we look at what's the right amount of growth for the tournament, and then if we're meeting that, what do we have that we can also be doing for the fan area? And this event is a good example where, we didn't shrink the competition to add more fan experience. And that was a dialogue. We looked at it. But first and foremost, it is the International Championships, it is to compete for Worlds as part of that overall narrative, so we wanted to make sure we served that audience first.

My hope is, resources allow, space allows, that we're able to create a fantastic experience for all of our fans, for all of our competitors, and there don't need to necessarily be trade-offs. Sometimes, when a space like this show happens, we have to focus on the competitor first just because that's the main goal of the event is a competition.

But our future planning is very much looking at how do we treat the two groups equally and provide support for fans, and when I say fans, I'm sort of thinking like people that don't really compete, they're not looking for the thrill of competition, for lack of a better word. But that can also be like, you're there, you're a parent with your kid and you just only have a couple of hours.

What is there to do on our show for 2, 3, 4 hours, and then you go home, and you had a great time, and you experienced Pokemon . So we do want to prioritize that. We do want to make that important going forward. It is important today, but we have this historical backbone that's a competition that we make sure to take care of.

Q: Do you anticipate Chicago being the home base for NAIC for the foreseeable future?

A: I will not make any comment after '27.

Pokemon Wants to Keep Competitive Play Accessible

Q: You really touched on the growth component with my first question. What has been like one of your favorite pieces of growth, whether it's the video games or new competitions, new formats. What have you enjoyed the most?

A: This is speaking more generally, not just to the show, because we're sort of seeing the growth across all the games, so I wouldn't say any one area has been like, oh my gosh, the highlight here is incredible. What I'd say is like, adding fan experience in general has been really interesting to figure out how to grow. So, we've got more carnival games here than we did last year. We're also adding that though across the circuit, so those are now coming up at a regional level.

Trying to just figure out what makes sense for somebody who, again, maybe isn't as engaged as a competitor would be, trying to figure out what's meaningful to them, what we should be adding. That's been, to me, the most surprising and also interesting thing for our team to work on. And we're still experimenting with it all the time.

We've not yet brought the programming stage to the NAIC event, but we have it at EUIC. And just trying to figure out, what do we put on that stage each hour is a challenge, one that has been rewarding in terms of sometimes we have things that really connect and others it's like, oh we want to learn from that we probably don't do that again. Fortunately, the fans that attend the event have been very accommodating and understanding as we try to figure out how to improve.

Probably more technical, just seeing how we can grow all of our systems. The logistics behind the event are insane. I forget who I was telling this to earlier, but we're deploying 1000 internet-connected stations, and we have under 48 hours to set that up, basically. That's an insane level of logistics, and we're doing that while we're also doing the Unite event that is 6 stations with 10 Nintendo Switches or mobile devices each, while we've got 64 stations that have 2 iPhones set up each while we're running 4 simultaneous streams where we're playing 5000 plus simultaneous TCG matches. Nowhere on the planet do these logistics exist. There's twice the number of people competing this weekend as the Winter Olympics has athletes. And we support them playing simultaneously, so the growth of just figuring out the staff, work structures, that's been super fascinating.

Q: This is a pivotal moment in terms of like 30th anniversary and going into Worlds. We've seen some of the stuff that's going to be revealed. How do you balance that idea of making it a significant moment for competitors this year but also making it a significant moment for the brand in its anniversary year?

A: That's been the hardest thing for us to juggle as a company is just. Fan fest and competition, and how do you position them. We made the decision early, in terms of planning PXP, we're going to try to position these two things equally. And you'll notice there are 2 websites. We doubled all of our work. That's been the approach, though, is we're doing our best for that show to balance a proper fan fest, everything that you should want for a fan, that you'd expect, I think we will have all of that. I don't want to reveal too much. If you're just there as a fan of competitive, all of those needs should still be serviced.

Q: Pokemon Champions is coming to mobile later this month. Will we ever see VGC competitions held on mobile devices or will cross-play be allowed?

A: We have more information coming later on the circuit. That's all I'll say.

Q: Every year, there are fans attending their first play. What do you hope they take away from that very first?

A: It kind of depends on where they are. I think, first of all, we have to think about our kids and their parents. Any parent that's coming with their kid, I want to make sure they've had a good time. If they're just coming, not to compete, but just to maybe do the Pikachu meet and greet, maybe they want to see Pokemon Center, maybe they want to go to the collect and trade. Whatever it is they're doing, we want to make sure that, ultimately, we're a brand that's about kids, we're a brand that's about families, and I want to make sure that that group, what are we doing for them? And there are little things we do, like hand out booster packs at random if we see a kid. There are opportunities to make sure, light touches that ensure they have a good time.

The competitor side is a little bit different. Most people's first event, competing is probably not the show. The reality is, on the competition side, if this is your first show, you're probably not winning. We do our best when it comes to the competitive side, though, try to nurture the spirit of the game, which is winning and losing with grace. We want the players to shake hands, we want people to respect each other when it comes to the competition, but we cannot guarantee that somebody wins and, actually, I'd say even the opposite. Everyone this weekend, except for maybe one or two people, will lose at some point.

So, it's really important that, for new attendees, if this were their first tournament, they need to understand both sides of that. Winning and losing in a way that shows good sportsmanship between each other. And that may not always be the most fun. For some people, losing is never fun. So, I can't guarantee what that experience is. If this is your first tournament and you 03 dropped in the TCG, I hope you had a great time. I hope you made 3 friends. And I hope you take losing as a learning experience.

Q: As you reflect on these 3 years in New Orleans, are there particular moments or things that you have evolved over those 3 years that you are proud of, that you look back on and say, I'm so glad we moved forward with this, and then how those maybe would influence ICs and Worlds moving forward.

A: Behind the scenes, we've really grown the overall company support for this event. There are more of us coming together, the staffing structures have all evolved, the show is a lot smoother than it was the first time we were here in New Orleans. So, operationally, we've made improvements every year, and it's getting more refined. For an event organizer, the best thing you can do is hold the same show in the same location at roughly the same time. That's the organizer's dream. We move Worlds around. Let me tell you, that's hard. That's really, really hard to do.

So, I'd say just very, very proud of how the organization, and that's not just TPCI, but all the partner companies that come together to put the show on, everyone's got to work together. We've got fantastic project management now across the whole show, and I'd say that 3 years ago, we did our best. We are 3 years more learning at being able to execute this show well, and I think attendees are maybe not even realizing it's happening, but we were joking earlier like it's been a smooth show so far. So, that's probably one of the big ones.

More publicly, I would say, and we're still learning, but I'd kind of point to the stage and the ceremonies. 4 years ago, we didn't have an opening ceremony at all. Then we came to New Orleans, and we were like, well, what do we do to add that extra engagement to share that local culture back to the fans, both here in person but also at home. So, we've experimented with the ceremonies, that's been a lot of learning there.

The stage has also evolved each year, so how we deploy the LEDs, how much LED there is, the lighting grid. So, there's been a huge evolution if you look at the stage 3 or 4 years ago to what it is today. But not just looking at it, what we're doing with it, because those LEDs, all programmable. So, like what are the cues we hit when something happens to make it engaging for people that are in the audience? Is there something that bleeds through to people that are watching at home?

We've had evolution in a few other areas. Collect and Trade, we rolled out for the first time here and that's been a big testing experience and getting that more refined each year. Pin trading, also new and rolled out in the last couple of years, which is getting stronger each year.

Registration queue management is another one that, if I look back 3 years ago to where we are now, it's so much smoother now. But New Orleans was special in that we could add airport badge pickup. I don't know the final number, but it was over 2000 people had picked up their badge at the airport by Wednesday night or Thursday morning. Which, those are 2000 people that aren't coming through and queuing and trying to get that stuff today, so that's, that's been big.

Q: Do you anticipate more people than usual to dip their toes into the VGC side of things because of the accessibility of Pokemon Champions ? And if so, how will you facilitate that growth?

A: Yes, we anticipate that. One, Champions on Switch 2 or Switch has been huge already. We sold this show out, so the demand's been incredible. The viewership growth has been huge too. Mobile, obviously, is just going to make that even more impactful, reach more fans, be more accessible.

In terms of what we're going to do in the future, we always try to project where the growth boundary is. There are logistical challenges with VGC specifically because of the networking, so we are trying to figure out, if there is some upper bound of what we can physically do, then what do we do with that? Do we prioritize registration? A lot of systems eventually evolve into these layers of qualification.

Subscribe to the newsletter for deeper Pokémon event insights

Looking for deeper coverage? Subscribe to the newsletter for detailed context on Pokémon events, competitive scene shifts, fan-accessibility developments, and behind-the-scenes perspective to follow how the scene evolves.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

We're trying to avoid that if we can, because those layers of qualifications end up creating accessibility windows. So it's like, oh I can only get into this at this time. If I miss this window, I have to wait, in traditional sports, that's often the whole year. I'm trying to avoid that if we can, so at the moment we don't have a great plan, a perfect plan. We don't know necessarily where the demand line's going to be.

We do plan to keep growing the size of the events, and at some point, though, if there's a logistical challenge, we'll attack it. We're not going to deploy a 4000-person network. It's just not going to happen. 2000 though? Yeah, we can get to 2000. We've proved we can do 1600 at UIC this year. So, somewhere between there we have to then figure out what else do we do for our qualification system to make it work. It's going to grow, and our job is to try to support the community.

Q: Could you describe the qualification systems?

A: We are an open tour, basically, so any one of our events other than Worlds, anyone can compete in without any sort of prerequisite other than they're in good standing, and they have a deck, or they have the equipment they need to participate. Most other qualification systems add layers. So, you want to compete at this layer, you need to qualify at this prior layer. And a lot of that's due to scale, so you want to maybe keep an event small. Well, to keep that small, I need to pre-qualify people. So, that's one approach to creating a system.

I think we benefit from a fantastic community that, in particular, is international, so our regional championships, even now, are averaging nearly 30 countries per event, and that's because we're an open circuit. At some point, does it become logistically impossible to run the event, and then you have to basically look at, do you create something that precedes that. Or we did this many, many years ago, over a decade ago for US nationals back when that was an event—we said you need to have like 5 play points, which are just participation points, if you want to register.

So, it could be as simple as something like that. I'm speculating right now. I'm not saying we're going to do this or this or this, but I will say, philosophically, the shows, at the size we are, we're seeing so much benefit for the community, and we don't want to lose that, but we don't want to lose the accessibility that we have. So, as much as we can, we want to try to be the most accessible eSport in the world.

Q: As you're talking about accessibility, there are a lot of people who are intimidated by card games. What do you think makes the Pokemon TCG approachable compared to what many people expect when they hear the phrase "competitive card game"?

A: I think we've got a lot going for the card game. I don't want to speak to others, but what we do really well, I think the first is we are considered the most accessible in terms of getting the cards you need for a competitive tournament. It's very approachable, and we create products too that give you a good base to then build and iterate a competitive deck from. So, the first thing is, for us, just building those decks is not a huge barrier to entry.

We also have things like TCG Live , which is extremely accessible. You can download it right now. It has tons of things that you can just immediately open up some booster packs and start making a deck. It also gives you preconstructed decks that it gives you that are actually competitive viable from your first day. So, at the very top of that funnel, we are the easiest TCG to get into. Easiest to make a deck for, easiest to play. So, that's one big strength.

Then, when it comes to our tournaments, we're the only group that is running 3 age divisions. Again, we're a family brand. We always think about kids. We want everyone to be a trainer. We want all kids to be trainers. The fact that we're actually supporting that in a way that a junior division—10, 11, 12 years old—you can be a world champion. You don't need to wait until you're an adult to become the world champion. You can do that as an 11-year-old, as a 10-year-old. So, that's a huge strength.

Finally, I'd say the backbone of our whole program, Pokemon leagues, I think we're in over 6 or 7,000 stores now globally, running local community Pokemon leagues, which then lead to league cups, league challenges, that's critical. Having that local community, building that local community, that makes us accessible. It makes it so that you don't have to get on a plane and fly to a regional to have your first experience. You have it in the store, in your hometown, maybe even with people you already know, hopefully your friends, getting you into the game.

The last thing I'd say is that we use the core of our premier events, the regional championships, internationals, worlds, the broadcast component, and how we're able to present the game in a way that no one else can really do right now. It's a physical card game that has all the advantages of presenting information digitally so you can follow them, and those become ways to learn the game, learn the meta, learn how to play a deck so that you can jump in at any time.

So, I downloaded TCG Live , and I got my first few booster packs. I built a deck in TCG Live , and I'm watching a YouTube video of how somebody piloted the latest big deck, and you can do all of that in a day. And it's super legible and what you're learning to do, it's very accessible, and if you're inspired from all of that, there's probably a local community within 10 minutes of you that you can join and play that weekend and meet new people.

[END]
item.content:encoded:
The Pokemon North America International Championships have become much more than a weekend for the best players in the world to chase a spot at Worlds. The 2026 NAIC took place in New Orleans from June 12–14, bringing Pokemon competitors and fans together near the end of the Championship Series season, but the event is already looking toward what comes next as The Pokemon Company prepares to move NAIC to Chicago.

GameRant participated in a group interview at this year's Pokemon NAIC with Chris Brown, Director of Global Esports and Events Producer for The Pokemon Company International. During the conversation, Brown discussed why Chicago gives NAIC room to grow, how Pokemon is balancing serious competition with family-friendly fan experiences, and why accessibility remains such a major part of the company's approach to competitive play.

The following transcript has been edited for brevity and clarity.

Why Pokemon NAIC Is Moving to Chicago

Q: With Pokemon 's footprint already big, with Gofest and the Fossil Museum, is the move to Chicago mainly floor space and capacity, or is there something about Chicago specifically that makes it really the place to be?

A: When we look at events, there are a lot of factors, and we look at airline access to that. New Orleans, by the way, has been fantastic for that. Chicago, just as good, maybe even better. Chicago is super accessible. Most places, major hubs around the world travel direct into Chicago. So that's a big part of this event. We have 48 countries and regions represented this weekend, for example, so having that accessibility is vital now for a show of this scale.

We look for things to do. Our fans, they come in for a competition, but what else is there for them to do? New Orleans, again, checks a ton of boxes. Chicago's very much the same, it's a world-class city. It's going to give our attendees so much to do beyond Pokemon , and we hope they come for the event and enjoy the show, compete and do their best, but at the end of the day, you need to get dinner somewhere. Chicago is a fantastic location that we think you could extend a vacation on either end of our event on.

We are also looking to grow. This venue, we had a 3-year deal, and I think I shared some of the growth metrics. We've been able to grow from when we moved to this venue for the first time 3 years ago to where we are now, we're up 150+%, in terms of what our attendance is. The shocking thing is we actually kind of reached our 3-year goal in 2 years. And so this year everything's sold out. We weren't really able to grow that event. It's going to be pretty flat year over year, not because the demand's not there. The demand is there.

We sold out a lot of our competitions in seconds, but we don't have the space. And it's not because this convention center doesn't have space. It's one of the largest convention centers in the world, really, especially for continuous space. But you have to get far enough out, book the halls, and the halls adjacent to us are booked by other clients. So, Chicago, for us, lets us grow the space, which, for us, we want to grow the competition. We also want to grow our fan programming.

So, everything we're doing that isn't for people that are competing, we want to continue to grow that. That's really where we've been limited for the last couple of years for this show. We've not been able to grow that fan programming because we've prioritized the competitive side and keeping that where it's at. Chicago checks all the boxes. Gives us growth for competitors, gives us growth for fans. It's a great vacation spot for families to attend, and it's extremely accessible for international guests.

Q: NAIC, next year, will come off of Worlds and XP. So, I'm curious if any of the sort of ideas that are going to be in play at Worlds and XP are going to be moved forward. Are there things that you're trying with Worlds and XP that will then become part of the IC identity?

A: We're always experimenting with events. We kind of go through that build, measure, learn loop, and, fortunately, we're able to test things at the ICs and bring them to Worlds and test things at Worlds and bring them to the ICs. I think the component that we've started testing at the international level that I think PXP is really going to break the most ground for is the exhibitor booth and the partner booth space.

If you were at EUIC, you may have seen for the first time we had this great Pokemon TCG Pocket booth. I'm not making any promises to what's at XP or anything, I'm just alluding to this concept of, these shows are so large now, this idea that there's booth space that our partners who are close to the brand can come in and activate and really meet the fan 1 to 1, it's a great experience for our partners. It's a great experience for our fans. So, I'm hoping that that's the next evolution of what we're able to offer at the IC level that isn't currently at this show.

Q: How do you balance creating a high-level competitive environment while still making the event welcoming for casual fans and families?

A: I can't ignore the history. For us, very fortunately, we've been running Pokemon for... we're a generational brand now. And that includes the World Championships, which TPCI first operated in 2004. So, the backbone of that event infrastructure starts from the competitive core, so we're fortunate in that we're grounded there, and then it really becomes a question of how we expand for the whole brand, for all fans, while staying true to what is important for our competitors.

In terms of balancing it, there's not a great answer, so much as we look at what's the right amount of growth for the tournament, and then if we're meeting that, what do we have that we can also be doing for the fan area? And this event is a good example where, we didn't shrink the competition to add more fan experience. And that was a dialogue. We looked at it. But first and foremost, it is the International Championships, it is to compete for Worlds as part of that overall narrative, so we wanted to make sure we served that audience first.

My hope is, resources allow, space allows, that we're able to create a fantastic experience for all of our fans, for all of our competitors, and there don't need to necessarily be trade-offs. Sometimes, when a space like this show happens, we have to focus on the competitor first just because that's the main goal of the event is a competition.

But our future planning is very much looking at how do we treat the two groups equally and provide support for fans, and when I say fans, I'm sort of thinking like people that don't really compete, they're not looking for the thrill of competition, for lack of a better word. But that can also be like, you're there, you're a parent with your kid and you just only have a couple of hours.

What is there to do on our show for 2, 3, 4 hours, and then you go home, and you had a great time, and you experienced Pokemon . So we do want to prioritize that. We do want to make that important going forward. It is important today, but we have this historical backbone that's a competition that we make sure to take care of.

Q: Do you anticipate Chicago being the home base for NAIC for the foreseeable future?

A: I will not make any comment after '27.

Pokemon Wants to Keep Competitive Play Accessible

Q: You really touched on the growth component with my first question. What has been like one of your favorite pieces of growth, whether it's the video games or new competitions, new formats. What have you enjoyed the most?

A: This is speaking more generally, not just to the show, because we're sort of seeing the growth across all the games, so I wouldn't say any one area has been like, oh my gosh, the highlight here is incredible. What I'd say is like, adding fan experience in general has been really interesting to figure out how to grow. So, we've got more carnival games here than we did last year. We're also adding that though across the circuit, so those are now coming up at a regional level.

Trying to just figure out what makes sense for somebody who, again, maybe isn't as engaged as a competitor would be, trying to figure out what's meaningful to them, what we should be adding. That's been, to me, the most surprising and also interesting thing for our team to work on. And we're still experimenting with it all the time.

We've not yet brought the programming stage to the NAIC event, but we have it at EUIC. And just trying to figure out, what do we put on that stage each hour is a challenge, one that has been rewarding in terms of sometimes we have things that really connect and others it's like, oh we want to learn from that we probably don't do that again. Fortunately, the fans that attend the event have been very accommodating and understanding as we try to figure out how to improve.

Probably more technical, just seeing how we can grow all of our systems. The logistics behind the event are insane. I forget who I was telling this to earlier, but we're deploying 1000 internet-connected stations, and we have under 48 hours to set that up, basically. That's an insane level of logistics, and we're doing that while we're also doing the Unite event that is 6 stations with 10 Nintendo Switches or mobile devices each, while we've got 64 stations that have 2 iPhones set up each while we're running 4 simultaneous streams where we're playing 5000 plus simultaneous TCG matches. Nowhere on the planet do these logistics exist. There's twice the number of people competing this weekend as the Winter Olympics has athletes. And we support them playing simultaneously, so the growth of just figuring out the staff, work structures, that's been super fascinating.

Q: This is a pivotal moment in terms of like 30th anniversary and going into Worlds. We've seen some of the stuff that's going to be revealed. How do you balance that idea of making it a significant moment for competitors this year but also making it a significant moment for the brand in its anniversary year?

A: That's been the hardest thing for us to juggle as a company is just. Fan fest and competition, and how do you position them. We made the decision early, in terms of planning PXP, we're going to try to position these two things equally. And you'll notice there are 2 websites. We doubled all of our work. That's been the approach, though, is we're doing our best for that show to balance a proper fan fest, everything that you should want for a fan, that you'd expect, I think we will have all of that. I don't want to reveal too much. If you're just there as a fan of competitive, all of those needs should still be serviced.

Q: Pokemon Champions is coming to mobile later this month. Will we ever see VGC competitions held on mobile devices or will cross-play be allowed?

A: We have more information coming later on the circuit. That's all I'll say.

Q: Every year, there are fans attending their first play. What do you hope they take away from that very first?

A: It kind of depends on where they are. I think, first of all, we have to think about our kids and their parents. Any parent that's coming with their kid, I want to make sure they've had a good time. If they're just coming, not to compete, but just to maybe do the Pikachu meet and greet, maybe they want to see Pokemon Center, maybe they want to go to the collect and trade. Whatever it is they're doing, we want to make sure that, ultimately, we're a brand that's about kids, we're a brand that's about families, and I want to make sure that that group, what are we doing for them? And there are little things we do, like hand out booster packs at random if we see a kid. There are opportunities to make sure, light touches that ensure they have a good time.

The competitor side is a little bit different. Most people's first event, competing is probably not the show. The reality is, on the competition side, if this is your first show, you're probably not winning. We do our best when it comes to the competitive side, though, try to nurture the spirit of the game, which is winning and losing with grace. We want the players to shake hands, we want people to respect each other when it comes to the competition, but we cannot guarantee that somebody wins and, actually, I'd say even the opposite. Everyone this weekend, except for maybe one or two people, will lose at some point.

So, it's really important that, for new attendees, if this were their first tournament, they need to understand both sides of that. Winning and losing in a way that shows good sportsmanship between each other. And that may not always be the most fun. For some people, losing is never fun. So, I can't guarantee what that experience is. If this is your first tournament and you 03 dropped in the TCG, I hope you had a great time. I hope you made 3 friends. And I hope you take losing as a learning experience.

Q: As you reflect on these 3 years in New Orleans, are there particular moments or things that you have evolved over those 3 years that you are proud of, that you look back on and say, I'm so glad we moved forward with this, and then how those maybe would influence ICs and Worlds moving forward.

A: Behind the scenes, we've really grown the overall company support for this event. There are more of us coming together, the staffing structures have all evolved, the show is a lot smoother than it was the first time we were here in New Orleans. So, operationally, we've made improvements every year, and it's getting more refined. For an event organizer, the best thing you can do is hold the same show in the same location at roughly the same time. That's the organizer's dream. We move Worlds around. Let me tell you, that's hard. That's really, really hard to do.

So, I'd say just very, very proud of how the organization, and that's not just TPCI, but all the partner companies that come together to put the show on, everyone's got to work together. We've got fantastic project management now across the whole show, and I'd say that 3 years ago, we did our best. We are 3 years more learning at being able to execute this show well, and I think attendees are maybe not even realizing it's happening, but we were joking earlier like it's been a smooth show so far. So, that's probably one of the big ones.

More publicly, I would say, and we're still learning, but I'd kind of point to the stage and the ceremonies. 4 years ago, we didn't have an opening ceremony at all. Then we came to New Orleans, and we were like, well, what do we do to add that extra engagement to share that local culture back to the fans, both here in person but also at home. So, we've experimented with the ceremonies, that's been a lot of learning there.

The stage has also evolved each year, so how we deploy the LEDs, how much LED there is, the lighting grid. So, there's been a huge evolution if you look at the stage 3 or 4 years ago to what it is today. But not just looking at it, what we're doing with it, because those LEDs, all programmable. So, like what are the cues we hit when something happens to make it engaging for people that are in the audience? Is there something that bleeds through to people that are watching at home?

We've had evolution in a few other areas. Collect and Trade, we rolled out for the first time here and that's been a big testing experience and getting that more refined each year. Pin trading, also new and rolled out in the last couple of years, which is getting stronger each year.

Registration queue management is another one that, if I look back 3 years ago to where we are now, it's so much smoother now. But New Orleans was special in that we could add airport badge pickup. I don't know the final number, but it was over 2000 people had picked up their badge at the airport by Wednesday night or Thursday morning. Which, those are 2000 people that aren't coming through and queuing and trying to get that stuff today, so that's, that's been big.

Q: Do you anticipate more people than usual to dip their toes into the VGC side of things because of the accessibility of Pokemon Champions ? And if so, how will you facilitate that growth?

A: Yes, we anticipate that. One, Champions on Switch 2 or Switch has been huge already. We sold this show out, so the demand's been incredible. The viewership growth has been huge too. Mobile, obviously, is just going to make that even more impactful, reach more fans, be more accessible.

In terms of what we're going to do in the future, we always try to project where the growth boundary is. There are logistical challenges with VGC specifically because of the networking, so we are trying to figure out, if there is some upper bound of what we can physically do, then what do we do with that? Do we prioritize registration? A lot of systems eventually evolve into these layers of qualification.

Subscribe to the newsletter for deeper Pokémon event insights

Looking for deeper coverage? Subscribe to the newsletter for detailed context on Pokémon events, competitive scene shifts, fan-accessibility developments, and behind-the-scenes perspective to follow how the scene evolves.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

We're trying to avoid that if we can, because those layers of qualifications end up creating accessibility windows. So it's like, oh I can only get into this at this time. If I miss this window, I have to wait, in traditional sports, that's often the whole year. I'm trying to avoid that if we can, so at the moment we don't have a great plan, a perfect plan. We don't know necessarily where the demand line's going to be.

We do plan to keep growing the size of the events, and at some point, though, if there's a logistical challenge, we'll attack it. We're not going to deploy a 4000-person network. It's just not going to happen. 2000 though? Yeah, we can get to 2000. We've proved we can do 1600 at UIC this year. So, somewhere between there we have to then figure out what else do we do for our qualification system to make it work. It's going to grow, and our job is to try to support the community.

Q: Could you describe the qualification systems?

A: We are an open tour, basically, so any one of our events other than Worlds, anyone can compete in without any sort of prerequisite other than they're in good standing, and they have a deck, or they have the equipment they need to participate. Most other qualification systems add layers. So, you want to compete at this layer, you need to qualify at this prior layer. And a lot of that's due to scale, so you want to maybe keep an event small. Well, to keep that small, I need to pre-qualify people. So, that's one approach to creating a system.

I think we benefit from a fantastic community that, in particular, is international, so our regional championships, even now, are averaging nearly 30 countries per event, and that's because we're an open circuit. At some point, does it become logistically impossible to run the event, and then you have to basically look at, do you create something that precedes that. Or we did this many, many years ago, over a decade ago for US nationals back when that was an event—we said you need to have like 5 play points, which are just participation points, if you want to register.

So, it could be as simple as something like that. I'm speculating right now. I'm not saying we're going to do this or this or this, but I will say, philosophically, the shows, at the size we are, we're seeing so much benefit for the community, and we don't want to lose that, but we don't want to lose the accessibility that we have. So, as much as we can, we want to try to be the most accessible eSport in the world.

Q: As you're talking about accessibility, there are a lot of people who are intimidated by card games. What do you think makes the Pokemon TCG approachable compared to what many people expect when they hear the phrase "competitive card game"?

A: I think we've got a lot going for the card game. I don't want to speak to others, but what we do really well, I think the first is we are considered the most accessible in terms of getting the cards you need for a competitive tournament. It's very approachable, and we create products too that give you a good base to then build and iterate a competitive deck from. So, the first thing is, for us, just building those decks is not a huge barrier to entry.

We also have things like TCG Live , which is extremely accessible. You can download it right now. It has tons of things that you can just immediately open up some booster packs and start making a deck. It also gives you preconstructed decks that it gives you that are actually competitive viable from your first day. So, at the very top of that funnel, we are the easiest TCG to get into. Easiest to make a deck for, easiest to play. So, that's one big strength.

Then, when it comes to our tournaments, we're the only group that is running 3 age divisions. Again, we're a family brand. We always think about kids. We want everyone to be a trainer. We want all kids to be trainers. The fact that we're actually supporting that in a way that a junior division—10, 11, 12 years old—you can be a world champion. You don't need to wait until you're an adult to become the world champion. You can do that as an 11-year-old, as a 10-year-old. So, that's a huge strength.

Finally, I'd say the backbone of our whole program, Pokemon leagues, I think we're in over 6 or 7,000 stores now globally, running local community Pokemon leagues, which then lead to league cups, league challenges, that's critical. Having that local community, building that local community, that makes us accessible. It makes it so that you don't have to get on a plane and fly to a regional to have your first experience. You have it in the store, in your hometown, maybe even with people you already know, hopefully your friends, getting you into the game.

The last thing I'd say is that we use the core of our premier events, the regional championships, internationals, worlds, the broadcast component, and how we're able to present the game in a way that no one else can really do right now. It's a physical card game that has all the advantages of presenting information digitally so you can follow them, and those become ways to learn the game, learn the meta, learn how to play a deck so that you can jump in at any time.

So, I downloaded TCG Live , and I got my first few booster packs. I built a deck in TCG Live , and I'm watching a YouTube video of how somebody piloted the latest big deck, and you can do all of that in a day. And it's super legible and what you're learning to do, it's very accessible, and if you're inspired from all of that, there's probably a local community within 10 minutes of you that you can join and play that weekend and meet new people.

[END]

item.title: New Pokemon Worlds and XP Details; NAIC 2027 Officially Confirmed for Chicago
item.description:
Later this year, the Pokemon competitive scene will descend on San Francisco for Pokemon Worlds 2026 and, for the first time, a fan-focused event called Pokemon XP. As part of the conclusion of this year’s North American International Champions, The Pokemon Company offered new details about Worlds and XP as well as confirming the venue for next year’s NAIC.

North American International Champions Headed to the Windy City

After a 3-year partnership with the city of New Orleans, NAIC is headed to Chicago in 2027. The annual North American leg of the tournament – which also includes trips to Europe for EUIC and Latin America for LAIC – will take over the McCormick Place Convention Center, which is near the coast of Lake Michigan and a touch south of the Chicago Bears’ home, Soldier Field. Coincidentally, Pokemon GO Fest was held not too far north of McCormick Place, at Grant Park.

It's also worth mentioning that between those two venues is the Field Museum, which is currently hosting the Pokemon Fossil Exhibit. Unfortunately, the Pokemon Fossil Museum is only slated to run until April 5, 2027, so unless the contract is extended or if The Pokemon Company has a secret announcement planned, the Fossil Museum will not be active during NAIC.

Still, I highly recommend visiting the Fossil Museum if you’re in the area, both to see the awesome exhibit and to pick up some rare Excavator Pikachus from the nearby raids and special research. And if you are still in the area for NAIC, it's a great stop as part of the trip. Check out Sue, the massive T-Rex fossil.

Chicago is a great pick for NAIC, which has been targeting family-friendly cities for its event for a while now. In talking to Director of Global Esports and Events Producer, Chris Brown, it sounds as though NAIC is looking to Chicago to offer the event’s next big step in terms of an event footprint and fan experiences. While most attend NAIC to compete, The Pokemon Company wants to ensure that those who attend also have plenty of things to see and do (and eat).

New Pokemon Worlds and XP Details Including Promo Cards

In addition to the Chicago NAIC reveal, TPCi also offered new details about this year’s Pokemon Worlds and Pokemon XP events in San Francisco this August. For those who may not be familiar, Pokemon Worlds is quite literally doubling this year, separating the competitive experience into Worlds as we know it and taking over the Chase Center. While Pokemon XP will be a fan-focused event that will feature vendors and experiences to entertain attendees who aren’t competing.

For those vendors, TPCi confirmed its partners for Pokemon Worlds and XP, including the debut of LEGO at the annual event. No doubt, there will be opportunities to see the current LEGO Pokemon sets up close and personal, as well as the Smart Play sets that will have released earlier in August.

Other vendors at Pokemon XP and Worlds include mainstays like Bandai Hobby, Build-A-Bear, and Santa Cruz. I’m most curious to see what Sideshow Collectibles has at the show, as their highly-detailed figures, replicas, and accessories are always incredible to see in person.

Competitive attendees to Pokemon Worlds will also get quite the welcome kit, which was just revealed at NAIC’s conclusion. Featuring what I’m going to refer to as crowned Pikachu, the backpack, playmat, card sleeves, and more will be available to any competitor at Worlds – a standard for larger Pokemon tournaments for a while now.

Subscribe for in-depth Pokemon Worlds & XP coverage

Join the newsletter for deeper, expert coverage of Pokemon Worlds, Pokemon XP and NAIC—analysis that unpacks event developments, competitive context, and what matters to both competitors and fans as these events evolve.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

But the real gem is the winners kit, which features a slick white with gold trim style. It’s an added bonus on top of the coveted trophy and prize money.

And finally, TPCi revealed some of the Promo Cards that will be available in the run up to Worlds at local tournaments, as well as the winners card for Worlds. As expected, Pikachu is the star here, and the design pays homage to Worlds and XP’s San Francisco home for 2026.

This is just the tip of the iceberg for Pokemon Worlds and XP, though. While we have a clearer picture of what the competitive scene will look like at Worlds, there is still much more to be revealed about Pokemon XP in terms of overall event schedule, exclusives, Pokemon GO tie-ins and more. TPCi is going big for Pokemon’s 30th Anniversary, and the Worlds/XP combination promises to be a major destination on the road through the rest of this year, so look for even more details about what this year’s events will offer soon.

item.title: 7 Open-World Masterpieces With Epic Stories
item.description:
I'll admit, I tend to be quite picky with open-world games. It takes something quite special to keep me engaged enough to want to seek out every little collectible and side story. Ghost of Yotei , for instance, had some of my favorite open-world sidequests I've ever experienced. This is because a lot of them were part of the broader story, while telling worthy, emotional ones of their own. As huge and ambitious as a world is, it can lack appeal without a suitably epic story tying it all together.

These games, for me, are more than just excellent open-world adventures. They give the journey an emotional impact, a gravity, and a reason to explore, by grounding it all in a great story.

Scratch & Peek

Identify the cover art while scratching off as little foil as possible.

Low reveal = bigger bonus

—

More Games
Scratch & Peek Identify the cover art while scratching off as little foil as possible.
EasyMediumHardPermadeath

Low reveal = bigger bonus
